exemplar.title: Eyewitness Testimony in Car Crashes - Reconstructive Memory IA

detailedExemplarData.title: Eyewitness Testimony in Car Crashes - Reconstructive Memory IA

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of investigation aim with real-world relevance
Accurate description of reconstructive memory and schema theory with linkage to IV
Fully operationalized null and research hypotheses
weaknesses: The theoretical discussion is descriptive rather than critically nuanced
Minor stylistic issues and lack of broader contextualisation
Relevance could be deepened by tighter connection between theory and practical question
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction robustly defines the aim, explains its relevance to eyewitness testimony, and operationalizes all variables in the hypotheses. Two strands merit top-band performance (hypotheses) and two strands are solid upper-band (aim and theory), justifying a 5 out of 6. Minor stylistic imprecision and a somewhat superficial theoretical discussion preclude the maximum mark.

strengths: Well-justified independent measures design with random allocation rationale
Detailed description and explanation of sampling technique and participant characteristics
Clear identification and control of key variables
weaknesses: Randomisation method and blinding procedures not specified
Choice of specific video and questionnaire materials not fully justified
Lack of demographic summary quantification for sample
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is thorough: design and sampling receive clear explanations aligned with the top band, and variable control is convincingly justified. The materials section, while described, lacks depth in rationale. Overall performance sits squarely in the highest band, meriting the full 4 marks.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and inferential statistics (means, SDs, MannâWhitney U)
Graphical presentation correctly labeled and addressing the hypothesis
Interpretation links statistical outcome to theoretical expectations
weaknesses: Exact p-values and effect-size measures are omitted
Graph lacks significance markers and sample-size annotation
Reliance on error-bar overlap rather than inferential emphasis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical methods are appropriate and generally well applied, and the graph supports the hypothesis. However, the absence of precise p-values, effect sizes, and significance indicators weakens the rigor. These omissions justify a one-mark deduction from the top band, resulting in 4 out of 6.

strengths: Findings linked to reconstructive memory and schema theory
Strengths and limitations clearly stated with relevance to validity and reliability
Modifications explicitly tied to limitations to enhance study rigour
weaknesses: Discussion lacks integration of wider literature or alternative frameworks
Limitations are not ranked by severity or broader generalisability
Justifications for modifications could further elaborate expected validity gains
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation cogently discusses results in light of the theoretical model, identifies and explains relevant strengths/limitations, and proposes justified modifications. The narrower scope of discussion and lack of broader context preclude the maximum. As such, performance aligns with the upper-mid band, earning 4 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar graph is correctly labelled, includes units and error bars (SD), and directly compares the two conditions relevant to the hypothesis.  Axis titles and a clear caption are present; however, the graph lacks indication of sample size and statistical significance marker, so a 5 (not 6) is most fitting.

Findings are explicitly interpreted: the null is rejected, the research hypothesis supported, and results linked back to the theoretical expectation of reconstructive memory.  The student comments on SD overlap and significance, demonstrating data engagement.  The discussion is accurate but not deeply analytic (e.g., no effect size), so a 5 is appropriate.

Both the null and research hypotheses are correctly written, identify the IV (verb severity: collided vs contacted) and DV (estimated speed in km/h), and each variable is explicitly operationalised.  No ambiguities remain, fully satisfying the top-band descriptor; hence the maximum 6 is justified.

Self-selected sampling with an opportunity back-up is described and the choice is justified in terms of practicality.  Participant characteristics (age, language proficiency) are detailed, and potential weaknesses (driving experience) are acknowledged, amounting to an explanation rather than mere listing.  Therefore a 4 is appropriate.

Reconstructive memory and schema theory are both described with sufficient detail (definitions, functions, and citations).  The student then explicitly uses these concepts to explain why a more intense verb should distort memory, showing the conceptual bridge between theory and the chosen IV.  While the account is accurate, the theoretical discussion is brief and somewhat descriptive rather than critically nuanced, meriting a 5 rather than 6.

The student relates the results to reconstructive memory and schema theory, explaining how verb severity altered recall.  The implications for EWT reliability are touched upon.  The discussion is clear but relatively brief and lacks integration of wider literature, aligning with the middle band at 4.

The student clearly states the investigationâs aim: to explore how verb severity in a leading question influences estimated speed in a car-crash video.  They go on to explain why this is relevant, linking the manipulation to the reliability of eyewitness testimony and potential police practices.  Although this explanation is concise, it explicitly connects the aim to real-world concerns and therefore fulfils the descriptor for an explained relevance.  Minor stylistic issues and a lack of broader contextualisation prevent a top-of-band award, so a solid 5 is appropriate.

Several relevant strengths (ethical consideration, independent measures, equal group sizes, environmental controls) and limitations (driving experience, unit familiarity, participant interaction) are not only identified but each is briefly explained in terms of its impact on validity or reliability, meeting the 5â6 descriptor.  The depth of explanation is good though not exhaustive, meriting a 5.

Key controls (single viewing, equal group sizes, standardised instructions) are identified with convincing justifications.  Materials are listed but the choice of the specific video and questionnaires is not explained in depth.  Because the explanation is stronger for controls than for materials, a mid-band 3 is warranted.

Two concrete modifications (screening for driving knowledge/unit familiarity; stricter enforcement of no-talking rule) are explicitly linked to the stated limitations and justified as ways to enhance validity.  While sound, the justifications remain concise rather than fully elaborated, placing the work at the lower end of the top band (5).

The student not only describes that an independent-measures design was used but also explains why (avoiding order effects and demand characteristics) and how random allocation was implemented.  This moves beyond description into explanation, matching the upper band.  The rationale is coherent and directly linked to validity, earning a 4.

Appropriate descriptive (means, SD) and inferential statistics (Mann-Whitney U for two independent groups, non-parametric assumptions) are applied, and the critical value comparison is correct.  Minor reporting omissions (exact p value, effect size) keep the work from perfection, so 5 is awarded.

annotations.comment: The description of reconstructive memory is overly general and lacks depth. The student should define key terms more precisely and provide a clearer explanation of how reconstructive processes operate within memory retrieval.
The relevance to eyewitness testimony is noted, but the student could strengthen this by explicitly linking the theoretical constructs to the practical research question in a concise rationale.
The aim mentions two verbs but does not justify why those specific levels of severity were chosen. Can the student clarify the selection criteria for collided versus contacted?
The rationale for participant recruitment is vague: the student should specify the planned sample size and demographics to demonstrate feasibility and representativeness.
The independent and dependent variables are clearly defined and operationalized, facilitating a straightforward test of the hypothesis.
Both the null and research hypotheses are operationalized with explicit IV and DV statements, satisfying the top-level requirement for hypothesis articulation.
The procedures list describes sampling and testing steps but omits details of how random allocation was implemented. Specify the randomization method to reinforce internal validity.
Include a statement on blinding procedures (e.g., whether administrators knew condition assignments) to strengthen control over experimenter bias.
The rationale for using an independent measures design is well articulated, with clear discussion of order effects and demand characteristics.
While the sampling technique is described, the student should justify how self-selection and opportunity sampling will produce a representative sample of eyewitnesses.
Provide a brief demographic summary (mean age, gender distribution) under Participants to enhance transparency of the sample.
Acknowledging driving experience variability is good, but the lack of quantification (e.g., number with licenses) weakens the limitationâs impact.
Specifying that the video was played only once shows thoughtful external validity control, mirroring real-life unpredictability.
The rationale for equal group sizes is clear, but the mechanism for ensuring parity (e.g., allocation checks) should be specified.
How were the standardized instructions tested for clarity or piloted? This step is vital to avoid misinterpretation.
The materials list adequately enumerates documents and tools but fails to justify the choice of video or questionnaire design.
The inclusion of mean and standard deviation in the descriptive section demonstrates solid understanding of variability.
The table clearly presents group means and SDs, facilitating transparent comparison of the two conditions.
Interpretation relies heavily on overlapping SD error bars rather than drawing on inferential results. The student should emphasize statistical findings over visual overlap.
Include an effect size measure (e.g., r for MannâWhitney) or confidence interval to quantify the magnitude of the difference.
The bar graph is correctly labelled and captioned, directly supporting the hypothesis comparison.
The graph lacks markers for statistical significance and does not indicate sample sizes on the axesâconsider adding these for clarity.
Annotate error bars with actual numerical values (SD or 95% CI) on the graph to strengthen quantitative interpretation.
The choice of MannâWhitney U for two independent groups and small sample size is well justified, demonstrating careful test selection.
The p-value is described as <0.05 but the exact p-value is not reported. Include the exact statistic to enhance precision.
Consider reporting an effect size (e.g., r) alongside the U value to convey the practical significance of the finding.
The discussion effectively links findings to reconstructive memory but omits consideration of alternative cognitive frameworks or potential confounds.
Integrate more secondary literature to contextualize results and demonstrate critical engagement with broader eyewitness research.
The strengths section robustly links procedural controls to validity and participant safety, demonstrating clear awareness of methodological rigor.
The limitations section lists relevant issues but does not prioritize those with the greatest impact on validity. Consider ranking limitations by severity.
To enhance evaluation of generalizability, discuss how findings might differ in real-world eyewitness samples beyond a high-school cohort.
Modifications are linked to limitations, but the student could further justify each change by explaining expected validity gains.
The conclusion succinctly links significance and theory support, reinforcing the studyâs core finding.
The conclusion omits discussion of study limitations and suggestions for future research, leaving the ending somewhat underdeveloped.
